exemplar.title: Does Ryanairâs environmental commitments pose a threat to its competitive model?

detailedExemplarData.title: Does Ryanairâs environmental commitments pose a threat to its competitive model?

subject.title: Business Management
subject.description:
**IB Business Management** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **business fundamentals, human resource management, finance and accounts, marketing, and operations management**, with a strong focus on **case study analysis, real-world business applications, and strategic decision-making**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|----------------|------------|--------|--------|---------------|
| **Paper 1** | Case study-based exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 25% |
| **Paper 2** | Core themes exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | Social enterprise case study | N/A | 1h 15min | HL: 25% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Written commentary (SL) / Research project (HL) | Various | Various | SL: 30%, HL: 20% |

strengths: Topic is communicated clearly and logically with concise history and definitions of key terms
Research question is explicitly stated, well-focused, and consistently linked to discussion
Methodology uses an appropriate range of relevant sources and models with clear justification
weaknesses: Minor layout issues in the table of contents and occasional digressions
Stakeholder mapping lacks a concrete matrix or diagram output
No explicit reflection on potential source bias in methodology
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place this work firmly in the top band for all three sub-strands (topic explanation, question focus, methodology), demonstrating clear, accurate communication, a well-focused question, and informed method selection.  Small lapsesâlayout details, missing stakeholder matrix, and bias reflectionâprevent a perfect 6, so a score of 5 accurately reflects the high but not flawless performance.

strengths: Demonstrates clear and coherent subject knowledge of airline economics, ESG concepts, and carbon accounting
Terminology and concepts are used accurately and consistently
Source material is mostly relevant and appropriately applied to support arguments
weaknesses: Some citation details are incomplete or truncated (e.g., Statista URL, âGreenly 2024â)
Reliance on secondary websites and limited triangulation between primary/secondary data
Minor typographical slips do not affect overall clarity but suggest proofreading gaps
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands (subject knowledge and terminology) meet top-band criteria, showing clear, coherent understanding and accurate usage.  Application of sources, while mostly appropriate, is occasionally superficial and lacks full citation rigorâplacing it in the upper middle band.  The mix of two top-band and one middle-band strands justifies a score of 5 out of 6.

strengths: Research draws on multiple quantitative and qualitative data sets consistently linked to the research question
Porterâs model and stakeholder analysis are systematically applied, yielding coherent strategic insights
Discussion weighs synergies and conflicts with some critical reflection on emissions metrics and methodological limitations
weaknesses: No primary research (e.g., interviews) limits depth and prevents highest marks
Sections on history and cost-cutting measures are descriptive rather than analytical
Evaluation lacks alternative scenarios or financial sensitivity analyses and deeper critique of source feasibility
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Research quality aligns with the lower end of the 7â9 band, analysis sits mid-band (4â6), and evaluation occupies the top of the 4â6 band.  Collectively, these strand performances support a mark at the lower threshold of the 7â9 band, so a holistic score of 7 out of 12 reflects strong critical thinking tempered by areas needing deeper, more original analysis.

strengths: Structure follows IB requirements with clear sections, logical headings, and complete elements
Layout and formatting (word count, title, figure labels, pagination) meet conventions
Overall presentation is neat and supports readability
weaknesses: List of Figures misaligned and overly narrow columns
Some figure captions and numbering are inconsistent with IB conventions
Minor typographical and formatting inconsistencies (e.g., blank tables, âcustomer haseâ)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All presentation criteria are met at the top-band level: appropriate structure, neat layout, and proper formatting.  Only minor issues with figure alignment and typos arise, which do not undermine the overall clarity.  Therefore, full marks (4/4) are warranted.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is explicitly stated (âDoes Ryanairâs environmental commitments pose a threat to its competitive model?â) and remains the focal thread throughout analysis, methodology and evaluation sections.  Discussion consistently returns to this question when interpreting data (e.g., emissions-intensity vs. total emissions).  The question is sufficiently narrow and operationalised via Porter and stakeholder tools, demonstrating strong focus.

The student constructs a reasoned discussion weighing synergies and conflicts between growth and net-zero targets, identifies stakeholder tensions, and recognises methodological limitations in the evaluation section.  There is some critical reflection on emissions-intensity metrics vs. absolute emissions.  Evaluation could be deepened with alternative scenarios or financial sensitivity testing, but the argument is nonetheless well-developed and partially critical, justifying the top of the 4â6 band.

The student demonstrates confident understanding of low-cost airline economics, ESG concepts, and carbon accounting protocols.  Explanations of Scope 1â3, emissions-intensity trade-offs, and cost-focus strategy are accurate and coherently linked to the argument.  Minor omissions (e.g., deeper financial ratio analysis) prevent full marks but overall knowledge is clear and effectively supported by sources.

Business-management terminologyâcost leadership, stakeholder influence, SAF, net-zero, carbon pricingâis used consistently and correctly.  Concepts are embedded naturally rather than name-dropped.  A handful of typographical slips (e.g., âcustomer haseâ) do not materially affect conceptual accuracy, so the upper band is justified.

The analysis of Porterâs model and stakeholder table is coherent and systematically applied to Ryanairâs context, yielding well-supported insights (e.g., fleet renewal effect on cost base).  Some sections remain descriptive (history of Ryanair, list of cost-cutting measures) and counter-arguments are not always fully explored, capping the strand at the middle of the 4â6 range.

Sources are generally well integrated (figures from Annual Reports, Statista, academic studies) and referenced.  However, some graphs lack full citation details, a few secondary websites (e.g., corporate blogs) are used without critical scrutiny, and there is limited triangulation between primary and secondary data.  Relevance remains clear, hence the higher middle band, but occasional superficial source use limits entry to the top band.

A wide range of recent corporate reports, academic journals and industry sources is used; selection is evidently purposeful (e.g., IATA roadmap, IPCC report, company annual reports).  MethodsâPorterâs generic strategies and stakeholder mappingâare justified in the methodology section and applied later.  Depth could be richer with additional analytical lenses (the student admits space prevented STEEPLE/SWOT), yet the evidence of informed selection and appropriateness meets the top band expectations.

Research draws on multiple quantitative data sets (passenger numbers, emissions scopes, revenue per passenger) and credible qualitative sources.  All are explicitly linked to the question.  While breadth is strong, depth of primary research (e.g., interviews, surveys) is absent, so the work aligns with the lower end of the 7â9 band rather than approaching 10â12.

Formatting is neat: word count supplied (3,986), title and date shown, tables/figures labelled, and academic referencing attempted in Harvard style.  Pagination is implied by figure references.  Occasional line-break anomalies and slight citation truncations exist, yet overall layout meets IB expectations and supports readability.

The student presents the topic in a clear, logical sequence beginning with a concise history of Ryanair, moving on to ESG context, and narrowing down to the precise issue of environmental commitments versus cost leadership.  Key terms (e.g., ESG, net-zero, cost focus) are defined and linked to the research focus, enabling a comprehensive reader understanding.  Minor lapsesâsuch as a slightly inflated table-of-contents layout and occasional digressionsâprevent a fully âcomprehensiveâ rating but overall clarity is strong.

The essay follows IB EE conventionsâtitle page, abstract substitute, contents list, numbered sections, introduction-analysis-evaluation-conclusion trajectoryâwithout missing elements.  Headings are logical and aid reader navigation.  Minor issues such as inconsistent figure numbering and an over-detailed contents list marginally detract but overall structure is clearly appropriate.

annotations.comment: The research question is explicitly and concisely stated, providing a strong, focused thread throughout the essay.
Word count is clearly provided and falls within the 4000-word limit, satisfying IB formatting requirements.
The title is clearly stated at the start, meeting the IB requirement for investigation title placement and enhancing report clarity.
The âList of Figuresâ page is formatted as a table with narrow columns, causing misalignment of entries; improve column widths for consistency.
The student cites consumer PR rankings without critically evaluating their reliability; this limits analytical depth.
The sustainability target percentages are stated without critique; discuss feasibility or assumptions behind these projections.
Mixing âtonsâ and metric units may confuse readers; standardize on SI units (e.g., âtonnesâ) throughout the essay.
The table of contents lacks page numbers for key sections (e.g., âIntroductionâ), which impedes navigation and clarity.
The excessively detailed contents list adds visual clutter; consider condensing entries to improve readability.
The historical narrative relies heavily on a 2015 student case; consider incorporating primary company sources or more recent academic studies.
The caption for Figure 1 appears above the graphic rather than below, deviating from IB EE conventions; reposition it for consistency.
The Statista URL in the figure source is truncated; provide full citation details and access date in Harvard style.
Link the environmental commitments discussion explicitly back to the research question to improve focus and coherence.
Citation âGreenly 2024â lacks full reference details at first mention; ensure complete Harvard citation immediately.
The legend text in Figure 2 is small and overlaps dark areas; increase font size and contrast to improve readability.
Quantify the statement that Scope 1 accounts for most emissions by including actual percentages from the figure.
The PMI definition of stakeholders is well integrated and accurately cited, demonstrating clear conceptual understanding.
Stakeholder mapping discussion lacks a concrete output; include a matrix or diagram showing influence vs importance for key groups.
Figure 4âs stakeholder list is cramped and hard to read; reformat the table with larger cells and clearer spacing.
Analysis correctly applies Porterâs cost focus to Ryanairâs model, yielding coherent insights into strategic fit.
The research question is restated verbatim from page 1, creating redundancy; synthesize instead to maintain flow.
The methodology justification clearly explains why Porterâs model and stakeholder analysis were selected and how they address the question.
Figure 3âs table lacks a case-specific discussion of cost focus; explicitly link each generic strategy to Ryanairâs context.
Table 6 is referred to without explicit numbering; add âTable 6:â before the caption for consistency.
The bullet list of costâcutting measures is clear and wellâstructured, effectively illustrating Ryanairâs operational tactics.
Placeholder tables on pages 16â17 appear empty; ensure any required data or remove blank elements to avoid confusion.
Continuation of the cost item list on the next page resets formatting; combine into a single unified list to maintain flow.
Figure 7 provides a useful comparison of route structures; consider citing a peerâreviewed source for academic rigor.
Table 1 is overly dense and spans the page width; break it into subâtables or summarize key stakeholders to aid comprehension.
Evaluation omits discussion of potential source bias; reflect on how selection of industry reports may have influenced findings.
Include the percentage increase calculation using an equation such as $$\frac{17,368,872-11,293,070}{11,293,070}\times100\%$$ to strengthen analysis.
Typographical error âcustomer haseâ should be corrected to âcustomer baseâ to maintain professionalism.
The conclusion summarizes findings but remains descriptive; add critical reflection on implications of SI targets versus absolute emissions.
References display inconsistencies in italics for journal and book titles; apply uniform Harvard formatting throughout.
Access dates in references use varying formats; standardize all to âday month yearâ to meet IB presentation requirements.
